title: 怪屋
summary:
Following a discussion with Inspector Taverner, the narrator's father and his son, Charles, analyze the death of Aristide Leonidis. The father observes that Aristide died dramatically within an hour of a specific conversation, a timing that prevented Roger Leonidis from developing greater suspicions. Taverner suggests that Brenda Leonidis is the primary beneficiary if a new will is not signed; if no new will exists, the old one remains effective, and if Aristide died intestate, his widow would inherit most of the estate. The father suspects trickery is involved in the matter of the will but cannot yet determine the method used.

Charles asks his father for a psychological profile of a murderer, as the case has become a dominant focus in his life. The father explains that murderers often appear to be good people, such as Roger, and are driven by obsession, jealousy, or necessity rather than simple hatred. He posits that victims are often people the killer loves and identifies "arrogance" as a universal trait of murderers, noting that their vanity often leads them to boast or discuss the case excessively to alleviate the isolation of their crime.

The father advises Charles to mingle with the Leonidis family to listen for unintentional slips of the tongue or self-revelations, noting that the family exhibits inherited arrogance. Finally, the father warns Charles to be careful with "that child," prompting Charles to ask if he should hide his intentions from Josephine.
